design_philosophy.core_loop: Turn-based galactic management: expand colonies, research technologies, build fleets, negotiate or wage war. Each turn represents resource production (minerals, organic, radioactive), research advancement, and military/diplomatic action resolution.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Multi-faction competition for galactic dominance with asymmetrical racial abilities and tech trees. Players balance expansion, military buildup, technological advancement, and diplomacy.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command an alien civilization to galactic conquest or peaceful dominion, with agency to choose combat, diplomacy, or technological superiority as the winning path.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes granular micromanagement and 'old-school' complexity. The game rewards obsessive attention to detail: planet resource optimization, facility placement, tech tree navigation, and fleet composition. It refuses accessibility in favor of depth, making it deliberately demanding for engaged players while excluding casual engagement.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant. Players configure galaxy size, number of opponents, opponent AI difficulty settings, and victory conditions (conquest, diplomacy, peaceful coexistence) at setup. Game rules permit multiple paths to victory (military, diplomatic, technological dominance). Computer opponents vary in difficulty: Xi-Chung aggressive early-game threat, Praetorian formidable late-game rival, Eee Consortium technologically superior. Difficulty emerges from AI behavior and asymmetrical racial advantages rather than scripted difficulty scaling.

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree with branching research lines. Each field (weapons, armor, engines, facilities, etc.) can be researched multiple times with escalating costs and benefits. Racial tech lines (Organic, Crystalline, Psychic, Temporal, Deeply Religious) unlock unique powerful components unavailable to other races.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Research completion gates access to new ship components, facilities, and research projects. Racial techs unlock powerful specialized options (Organic Armor, Religious Talisman, Allegiance Subverter).
replayability_hooks: Asymmetrical racial selection with distinct strengths and tech trees
Procedurally-generated galaxy layout with variable star systems, planets, warp point connectivity
Multiple victory conditions (military conquest, diplomacy, peaceful coexistence)
Computer opponent behavior variation (aggressive, expansionist, peaceful, technologically-focused)
Player-configurable game setup (galaxy size, opponent count, resource distribution)
High technological variation between empires due to large tech tree branching

world_building_techniques: Procedural environment storytelling: star system generation (supernova-prone stars, warp networks), planetary conditions (atmosphere types, mineral content, habitability)
Racial characterization through mechanical behavior: Praetorians as expansionist-peaceful, Eee Consortium as intellectually superior, Xi-Chung as aggressive-early-focused

Technological narrative: research tree progression implies civilization development arc from early industrial to reality-warping late-game (planetary destruction, star manipulation creating black holes and storms)
Implicit faction identity: racial techs embed cultural difference (Organic, Crystalline, Psychic, Temporal, Religious pathways)

faction_indicators: Player-selected alien race with predefined strengths and tech tree access
Racial tech specialization (Organic, Crystalline, Psychic, Temporal, Deeply Religious) as identity markers
Diplomatic vs. military playstyle as social signal (peaceful coexistence vs. conquest victory)
danger_signals: Xi-Chung Hive's aggressive early-game military dominance
Eee Consortium's technological superiority emerging in late game
Praetorian Empire's expansionist tendencies becoming formidable threat by mid-to-late game
Military fleet composition as threat assessment indicator
environmental_storytelling: Galaxy topology: warp point networks define strategic chokepoints and expansion routes
Star system hazards (supernova-prone stars, spatial storms) create danger zones and strategic planning pressure
Planetary habitability variation (ice/rock/gas, atmosphere types, resource distribution) shapes colonization desirability and reveals empire priorities
Resource scarcity patterns (mineral, organic, radioactive distribution) force strategic expansion directions
